content.text: Without a formal model registry, engineers select AI models ad hoc—picking whatever is cheapest, newest, or most familiar. This creates a shadow AI estate where the organisation does not know what models process what data, which contracts cover which deployments, or whether approved security and compliance reviews have been completed. The approved model registry is the governance artefact that brings order to model selection across the enterprise.

An approved model registry is a curated catalogue of AI models that have been reviewed, approved, and classified for use within the enterprise. Each registry entry records: the model identifier and version, the vendor, the usage tier (general, reasoning, code, PII-sensitive), permitted data classification levels, associated DPA and security review references, cost parameters, and deprecation status. Agents and developers must select models from the registry; use of models outside the registry requires an exception process.

Model proliferation is a governance risk. Without a registry, an engineer can add a new model vendor to a production agent with no security review, no DPA, and no cost tracking. Regulators increasingly expect organisations to maintain records of AI systems used in decision-making (GDPR Article 22, EU AI Act). A registry is the operational foundation of this record-keeping, and it doubles as an operational efficiency tool: centralised review amortises compliance work across all teams rather than each team repeating it independently.
Processing PII, financial, or health data
Highest-sensitivity data; must not leave enterprise network
date the security and compliance review was completed.
date the current approval expires (typically 12 months).
DPA document identifier in the legal system.
maximum context window size for capacity planning.
committed rate per million tokens for FinOps tracking.
Handling model family updates vs version-level approvals

AI vendors release new model versions frequently—sometimes monthly. A blanket approval for a model family (e.g. 'all Claude 3 models') creates ambiguity: does a new 3.7 release require a fresh approval? Adopt a clear policy: tier and data classification approvals are granted at the model family level; version-specific approvals are required only when a vendor announces breaking changes to model behaviour, safety profile, or API schema. For routine version updates within a family, require only a change notification to the registry owner within 5 business days, who must confirm no breaking changes before updating the registry to include the new version. This approach balances governance rigour with the operational reality of frequent model releases.

Because every approved model has a cost_per_Mtok field in the registry, your FinOps pipeline can look up the effective cost of each API call by querying the registry for the model_id in the request. This enables automatic cost attribution: when agent code logs the model_id used for each request, the FinOps service joins on the registry to compute cost and attributes it to the requesting workflow and cost centre. Without the registry as a central cost reference, FinOps teams must maintain a separate pricing table that quickly diverges from actual contracted rates as DPAs are renegotiated.
Sunset and deprecation tracking in the registry

Models are deprecated by vendors and by your own governance team. Track two types of deprecation in the registry: vendor-initiated (the vendor announces end-of-life for a model version) and enterprise-initiated (your governance team decides to phase out a model due to quality, cost, or compliance reasons). For vendor-initiated deprecations, the registry should reflect the vendor's announced deprecation date and the migration target model. For enterprise-initiated deprecations, the registry should record the decision rationale and the enforcement date after which the model is blocked in CI/CD. Notify all owners of agent workflows using the deprecated model 90 days before the enforcement date.
send notification to all workflow owners using the deprecated model.
review migration progress; escalate stalled migrations to platform steering committee.
enable CI/CD hard block to prevent new deployments of the deprecated model.
block all runtime calls; remaining callers route to the migration target model.
Registry governance and steering committee oversight

The approved model registry should be governed by a cross-functional committee: AI platform engineering (evaluates model capability and integration requirements), security (reviews safety and access controls), legal/privacy (reviews DPA status and data classification alignment), and finance (validates cost parameters). Convene this committee monthly for routine approvals and on an emergency basis for urgent registry updates (e.g. a security vulnerability in an approved model that requires immediate deprecation). Maintain a governance log of all committee decisions as an audit artefact, including the rationale for both approvals and rejections. This structured governance distinguishes mature AI platform programmes from ad hoc model adoption practices.

attrs.alt: Approved model registry tiers and access control by tier

content.text: As the gating mechanism for any new model usage in production agent workflows.
During security or compliance audits to demonstrate model governance.
For FinOps tracking: all model spend should tie back to registry entries.
When onboarding new engineering teams who need to select models for their workflows.
Inventory all models currently in use across the organisation (query cloud billing APIs and code repositories).
Create a registry entry for each in-use model and classify it into the tier structure.
Run a retrospective review for any model without a completed security review or DPA.
Build a registry lookup tool (CLI or API) that developers can query to find approved models for their data classification.
Configure your CI/CD pipeline to check that model IDs in agent code exist in the registry before deployment.
Schedule annual re-review of all registry entries and automated deprecation notifications at 60 days before expiry.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Literal
import json

@dataclass
class RegistryEntry:
    model_id: str
    vendor: str
    tier: Literal["T1","T2","T3","T4","T5"]
    approved_date: str
    review_expiry: str
    dpa_reference: str | None
    max_context_tokens: int
    cost_per_Mtok_input: float
    cost_per_Mtok_output: float
    status: Literal["approved","deprecated","under_review"]
    permitted_data_classes: list[str]

REGISTRY: list[RegistryEntry] = [
    RegistryEntry(
        model_id="claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022",
        vendor="Anthropic", tier="T4",
        approved_date="2025-01-10", review_expiry="2026-01-10",
        dpa_reference="DPA-ANT-2025-001",
        max_context_tokens=200_000,
        cost_per_Mtok_input=3.0, cost_per_Mtok_output=15.0,
        status="approved",
        permitted_data_classes=["public","internal","confidential","restricted"],
    ),
    RegistryEntry(
        model_id="gpt-4o-2024-11-20",
        vendor="OpenAI", tier="T3",
        approved_date="2025-02-15", review_expiry="2026-02-15",
        dpa_reference="DPA-OAI-2025-002",
        max_context_tokens=128_000,
        cost_per_Mtok_input=2.5, cost_per_Mtok_output=10.0,
        status="approved",
        permitted_data_classes=["public","internal","confidential"],
    ),
]

def lookup(data_class: str, tier: str | None = None) -> list[RegistryEntry]:
    """Return approved models permitted for the given data classification."""
    return [
        e for e in REGISTRY
        if e.status == "approved"
        and data_class in e.permitted_data_classes
        and (tier is None or e.tier == tier)
    ]

for e in lookup("confidential"):
    print(f"{e.model_id} ({e.tier}): ${e.cost_per_Mtok_input}/Mtok-in")

A global bank discovered that 14 different AI models from 7 vendors were in use across its technology teams, with no central record of which models had DPAs, security reviews, or compliance approvals. After building an approved model registry, the CISO discovered that two models were processing customer financial data without a completed DPA. These were immediately quarantined pending compliance remediation, and the registry became the single source of truth for all future model procurement decisions.
a PDF or spreadsheet that is not enforced in CI/CD is soon ignored; build the registry as a queryable API.
model behaviour changes between versions; track version-level approvals, not just model family approvals.
security landscapes change; models approved two years ago may no longer meet current standards.
models not in the registry should be blocked by default, not allowed; an allowlist is safer than a blocklist.
Expose the registry as a REST API so CI/CD pipelines can validate model IDs before deployment.
Auto-notify model owners 60 days before their model's review expires.
Require a registry entry for any model used in a production agent, even for open-source locally-hosted models.
Include cost-per-token in the registry so FinOps teams can attribute model spend accurately.

The registry itself is a lightweight artefact—hundreds of entries at most. The operational overhead is in the review process that populates it. Scale the review process by defining standard review templates per vendor tier: a pre-approved template for models from vendors already on the approved vendor list requires only a delta review of the specific model version's changes; a new vendor model requires a full review. This tiered review process keeps the registry current without creating a review bottleneck as the organisation adopts new models.

Integrate the registry with your developer portal and IDE tooling. A VS Code extension that lists approved models when a developer types a model ID string in code, and warns when a non-registry model is used, makes compliance the path of least resistance. Similarly, a pre-commit hook that checks model IDs against the registry API prevents unapproved models from reaching production code review. These tooling integrations shift compliance left—catching governance gaps at development time rather than in audit.

Each registry entry should have a named owner—the team or individual responsible for maintaining the model's review and DPA. When the model version is updated, the owner is responsible for initiating a delta review and updating the registry. When the vendor changes DPA terms, the owner is the first point of contact. This ownership model prevents the registry from becoming a maintenance burden with no clear accountability. Include model ownership assignment as part of the model approval workflow so every entry has an owner from day one.
Registry storage with version control and audit trail
Validate model IDs in agent code against the registry at build time
Developer-facing registry lookup at code-write time
Track registry coverage, expiry dates, and pending reviews
fast initially; creates untracked compliance exposure; not viable at enterprise scale.
simpler than a tiered registry; does not capture data classification requirements or version-level approvals.
External AI governance platform (e.g. Credo AI, Arthur):
provides registry plus model monitoring; adds a third-party dependency and cost; appropriate for organisations with dedicated AI governance teams.

The registry is a security control: it prevents unapproved models from processing sensitive data. Protect the registry itself with access controls: read access for all engineers, write access only for the AI governance team. Maintain an immutable audit log of all registry changes (approvals, deprecations, tier changes) so you can demonstrate to regulators that model governance was applied consistently and that any deviation was reviewed and documented.
Build the approved model registry as a queryable API, not a static document, so it can be enforced in CI/CD.
Define data classification tiers in the registry so model selection is driven by data sensitivity, not just cost or capability.
Assign a named owner to each registry entry who is responsible for review renewals and DPA updates.
Integrate the registry with developer tooling to make compliant model selection the path of least resistance.
Track model version-level approvals; a new minor version of an approved model requires at least a delta review.
